title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.1 The Core Intuition: Why Time Matters
Welcome to the first module of Business Finance Foundations! In this module, we will explore one of the most fundamental concepts in finance: the Time Value of Money (TVM). Understanding TVM is crucial because it underpins virtually every financial decision you’ll make, from personal savings to corporate investment analysis.
If I offered you $1,000 today or $1,000 one year from today, which would you choose?
Almost everyone instinctively chooses the money today. But why? In finance, this isn’t just a preference; it’s a fundamental principle called the Time Value of Money (TVM).
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.1.1 The Three Pillars of TVM
1. Opportunity Cost: The “Earning Power” of Money
Formal Definition: Opportunity cost represents the return you forgo by not having money available to invest today.
In practice: If you have $1,000 today, you could invest it in a savings account, bonds, or stocks and earn a return. By waiting a year to receive that $1,000, you lose the opportunity to grow that money during the waiting period.
Example: If you could earn 5% in a savings account, having $1,000 today means you’d have $1,050 in one year. Waiting to receive $1,000 next year costs you that $50 in potential earnings.
Real-World Application: This is why lottery winners often choose lump-sum payments over annuities, and why companies evaluate projects based on their opportunity cost of capital.

2. Inflation: The “Purchasing Power” Erosion
Formal Definition: Inflation is the general increase in prices over time, which reduces the real purchasing power of money.
In practice: A dollar today buys more groceries, gas, or coffee than a dollar will next year because prices tend to rise over time.
Example: If inflation is 3% annually: - A $100 grocery basket today will cost $103 next year - Your $100 next year will only buy what $97.09 buys today - Over 10 years, that $100 will only have the purchasing power of about $74.41 in today’s terms
Historical Context: The U.S. has experienced average annual inflation of about 3% over the past century. During the 1970s, inflation reached double digits, dramatically illustrating how quickly purchasing power can erode.

3. Risk (Uncertainty): The “Will I Actually Get Paid?” Factor
Formal Definition: Risk represents the probability that promised future cash flows may not materialize as expected.
In practice: There’s always some chance that the person or company promising to pay you in the future might not be able to deliver. They could go bankrupt, default, or circumstances could change. Money in your hand today has zero “default risk.”
Example: Would you rather have $1,000 today from a friend, or a promise that your friend will pay you $1,000 in 5 years? Even if you trust your friend, a lot can happen in 5 years—job loss, relocation, financial hardship. The promise carries risk.
Financial Markets Application: This is why U.S. Treasury bonds (backed by the government) pay lower interest rates than corporate bonds—Treasuries have virtually no default risk, while corporations might fail.



2.1.2 Understanding the Discount Rate (Interest Rate)
The interest rate we use in TVM calculations isn’t arbitrary—it reflects these three components:
Discount Rate = Risk-Free Rate + Inflation Premium + Risk Premium

Risk-Free Rate: The “pure” time value of money—what you’d earn with zero risk (typically approximated by U.S. Treasury securities, currently around 2-4%)
Inflation Premium: Compensation for expected loss of purchasing power (historically ~2-3% in the U.S.)
Risk Premium: Extra return demanded for bearing uncertainty (varies widely: 1-2% for high-quality corporate bonds, 5-8% for stocks, 10%+ for venture capital)

Example: If you’re evaluating a corporate bond investment: - Risk-free rate: 3% (what Treasury bonds pay) - Expected inflation: 2% - Corporate risk premium: 2% - Total discount rate: 7%
This is why riskier investments require higher returns—investors demand compensation for bearing additional uncertainty.

Deep Dive: The Risk-Free Rate
In theory, the risk-free rate represents the return on an investment with zero default risk and zero reinvestment risk. In practice, we use short-term U.S. Treasury bills as a proxy because: - The U.S. government can print money to pay debts (no default risk) - Treasury bills mature in less than one year (minimal interest rate risk)
However, even Treasuries aren’t perfectly “risk-free”—they still carry inflation risk and the theoretical possibility of government default.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.2 The “Magic” of Compounding
Compounding is the process where your money earns interest, and then that interest earns interest. This creates exponential growth rather than linear growth.

2.2.1 Simple Example
Imagine you invest $100 at a 10% annual interest rate:

Year 1: You earn $10 in interest. Your total is $110.
Year 2: You don’t just earn interest on your original $100; you earn 10% on the full $110. You earn $11 in interest. Your total is $121.
Year 3: You earn 10% on $121 = $12.10. Your total is $133.10.

Notice how the interest earned each year increases. That extra $1 in Year 2 and $2.10 in Year 3 is the result of compounding—earning interest on your interest.


2.2.2 The Power of Time
This “interest on interest” effect is what allows small savings to grow into large sums over long periods. Consider this dramatic example:
$10,000 invested at 8% annual interest: - After 10 years: $21,589 - After 20 years: $46,610 - After 30 years: $100,627 - After 40 years: $217,245
Notice that the growth accelerates over time. The fourth decade adds more than $116,000, while the first decade only adds about $11,500. This is the exponential nature of compounding.

Historical Example: Manhattan Island
The famous story of Manhattan being purchased for $24 in 1626 illustrates compounding’s power. If that $24 had been invested at 8% annual return, it would be worth over $200 trillion today—far more than Manhattan’s actual value. While this is a simplified example (finding 8% returns for 400 years is impossible), it demonstrates why starting early matters so much.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.3 Future Value (FV) vs. Present Value (PV)
In this module, we focus on two primary directions of calculation:

Future Value (FV): Moving money forward in time. “If I save $X today, what will it be worth in the future?”
Present Value (PV): Moving money backward in time (often called Discounting). “If I need $X in the future, what is it worth to me today?”


2.3.1 The Basic Variables and Notation
To solve any TVM problem, we work with these four core variables. We’ll use consistent notation throughout this course:

PV (Present Value): The current value of money; what a sum is worth today
FV (Future Value): The value of money at a specific point in the future
r (Interest Rate): The percentage rate of return per period (also called discount rate). In Excel, this parameter is called rate.
n (Number of Periods): How long the money is invested or borrowed. In Excel, this parameter is called nper.

Key Principle: If you know any three of these variables, you can solve for the fourth. This is the foundation of all TVM calculations.


2.3.2 Visual Timeline
        PV                                    FV
         |------------------------------------>|
       Today    n periods at rate r        Future
    
    Compounding: Move PV forward to find FV
    Discounting: Move FV backward to find PV
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.4 Simple vs. Compound Interest

2.4.1 Simple Interest
Definition: Interest is calculated only on the original principal amount. Interest earned in previous periods does not earn additional interest.
Formula: \[\text{Simple Interest} = \text{Principal} \times \text{Rate} \times \text{Time}\]
Example: $1,000 at 10% simple interest for 5 years: - Year 1: Earn $100 (total: $1,100) - Year 2: Earn $100 (total: $1,200) - Year 3: Earn $100 (total: $1,300) - Year 4: Earn $100 (total: $1,400) - Year 5: Earn $100 (total: $1,500)
Total interest earned: $500



2.4.2 Compound Interest
Definition: Interest is calculated on the principal plus all accumulated interest from previous periods. This creates “interest on interest.”
Formula: \[FV = PV \times (1 + r)^n\]
Example: $1,000 at 10% compound interest for 5 years: - Year 1: Earn $100 on $1,000 (total: $1,100) - Year 2: Earn $110 on $1,100 (total: $1,210) - Year 3: Earn $121 on $1,210 (total: $1,331) - Year 4: Earn $133.10 on $1,331 (total: $1,464.10) - Year 5: Earn $146.41 on $1,464.10 (total: $1,610.51)
Total interest earned: $610.51
The Difference: Compound interest earned $110.51 more than simple interest over just 5 years. Over longer periods, this difference becomes enormous.


2.4.3 Which One is Used in Modern Finance?
Compound interest is the standard in virtually all modern financial applications, including: - Savings accounts and CDs - Bonds and loans - Stock market returns - Retirement accounts - Mortgage calculations
Simple interest is rarely used except in very short-term loans or specific legal contexts.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.5 The Impact of Compounding Frequency

2.5.1 How Often Interest is Calculated Matters
Formal Definition: Compounding frequency refers to how many times per year interest is calculated and added to the principal.
In practice: The more frequently interest is calculated and added to your account, the more “interest on interest” you earn, even if the stated annual rate is the same.


2.5.2 Common Compounding Frequencies
Here’s how $1,000 grows at 6% annual interest for 1 year under different compounding frequencies:

Key Insight: Notice that going from annual to monthly compounding adds $1.68, but going from monthly to daily only adds $0.15. The benefit of more frequent compounding diminishes as frequency increases. There’s a mathematical limit (continuous compounding) beyond which you can’t go.


2.5.3 Excel Adjustment for Different Frequencies
When compounding is not annual, you must adjust both the rate and the number of periods:
Monthly compounding example: - Annual rate: 6% → Monthly rate: 6%/12 = 0.5% = 0.005 - Years: 5 → Months: 5 × 12 = 60
Excel formula:
=FV(0.06/12, 5*12, 0, -1000)
Result: $1,348.85 (compared to $1,338.23 with annual compounding)


2.5.4 APR vs. APY: What Banks Don’t Always Make Clear
APR (Annual Percentage Rate): The stated annual rate before accounting for compounding frequency. This is the “nominal” rate.
APY (Annual Percentage Yield): The effective annual rate after accounting for compounding frequency. This is what you actually earn.
Formula: \[APY = \left(1 + \frac{APR}{m}\right)^m - 1\]
Where m = number of compounding periods per year
Example: - 6% APR compounded monthly - APY = (1 + 0.06/12)^12 - 1 = (1.005)^12 - 1 = 0.06168 = 6.168%
Why This Matters: When comparing savings accounts or credit cards, always compare APY, not APR. A 5.9% APR compounded daily might be better than a 6.0% APR compounded annually.

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.6 The Mathematical Foundation

2.6.1 Future Value Formula
The fundamental formula for calculating future value with compound interest is:
\[FV = PV \times (1 + r)^n\]
Where: - \(FV\) = Future Value - \(PV\) = Present Value - \(r\) = Interest rate per period (as a decimal) - \(n\) = Number of periods
Example: What will $5,000 be worth in 10 years at 7% annual interest?
\[FV = 5,000 \times (1.07)^{10} = 5,000 \times 1.96715 = \$9,835.76\]



2.6.2 Present Value Formula
To find present value (discounting), we rearrange the future value formula:
\[PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r)^n}\]
This can also be written as:
\[PV = FV \times (1 + r)^{-n}\]
Example: What is the present value of $10,000 to be received in 8 years if the discount rate is 6%?
\[PV = \frac{10,000}{(1.06)^8} = \frac{10,000}{1.59385} = \$6,274.12\]
Interpretation: You should be willing to pay $6,274.12 today for the right to receive $10,000 in 8 years, assuming a 6% discount rate.



2.6.3 The Relationship Between PV and FV
These formulas are inverses of each other: - Compounding (FV): Multiply by \((1 + r)^n\) - Discounting (PV): Divide by \((1 + r)^n\) (or multiply by \((1 + r)^{-n}\))
Key Insight: When interest rates are positive, PV is always less than FV. The higher the interest rate or the longer the time period, the greater the difference between PV and FV.

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
section: 2.7 Financial Mental Models: The Rule of 72
text:
2.7 Financial Mental Models: The Rule of 72

2.7.1 Quick Estimation Tool
The Rule of 72 is a mental math shortcut used by financial professionals to estimate how long it takes for an investment to double at a given interest rate.
The Formula: \[\text{Years to Double} \approx \frac{72}{\text{Interest Rate (as whole number)}}\]
Examples: - At 6% interest: 72 ÷ 6 = 12 years to double - At 8% interest: 72 ÷ 8 = 9 years to double - At 9% interest: 72 ÷ 9 = 8 years to double - At 12% interest: 72 ÷ 12 = 6 years to double


2.7.2 Verification
Let’s verify the 8% example: \[FV = PV \times (1.08)^9 = 1 \times 1.999 \approx 2.0\]
The Rule of 72 predicted 9 years, and the actual calculation shows 1.999—remarkably accurate!


2.7.3 Why It Works (The Math Behind It)
The Rule of 72 is a linear approximation of the logarithmic formula for doubling time:
\[n = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1 + r)} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}\]
When r is expressed as a percentage, 0.693 × 100 ≈ 69.3, but 72 is used because it has more divisors (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 12, 18, 24, 36, 72), making mental math easier.
Accuracy: The Rule of 72 is remarkably accurate for interest rates between 6% and 10%. For rates outside this range, small errors accumulate, but it remains useful for quick estimates.


2.7.4 Reverse Application
You can also use the Rule of 72 in reverse: “If I want to double my money in 6 years, what return do I need?”
\[\text{Required Rate} = \frac{72}{\text{Years}} = \frac{72}{6} = 12\%\]


2.7.5 Related Rules

Deep Dive: Related Rules

Rule of 115: Estimates when money triples (115 ÷ rate)

At 8%: 115 ÷ 8 ≈ 14.4 years to triple

Rule of 144: Estimates when money quadruples (144 ÷ rate)

At 8%: 144 ÷ 8 = 18 years to quadruple


These are all linear approximations of exponential growth—handy for quick boardroom calculations when you don’t have a calculator.



2.7.6 Practical Applications
Retirement Planning: “I have $100,000 in my 401(k) at age 40. If it grows at 9% annually, how much will I have at 64?” - 24 years ÷ 8 years per doubling (72÷9) = 3 doublings - $100,000 → $200,000 → $400,000 → $800,000 - Quick estimate: ~$800,000 (actual: $806,862)
Inflation Impact: “If inflation is 3%, how long until my purchasing power is cut in half?” - 72 ÷ 3 = 24 years - A dollar today will have the purchasing power of 50 cents in 24 years

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.8 Moving to Excel
While formulas are important for understanding the logic, in the business world, we use Excel to handle calculations efficiently and accurately. Excel simplifies these calculations into built-in financial functions.

2.8.1 Core Excel TVM Functions

=FV(rate, nper, pmt, [pv], [type]) — Calculate future value
=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type]) — Calculate present value
=RATE(nper, pmt, pv, [fv], [type]) — Calculate interest rate
=NPER(rate, pmt, pv, [fv], [type]) — Calculate number of periods



2.8.2 Important Excel Conventions and Defaults
1. Cash Flow Sign Convention
Excel uses a cash flow perspective where: - Negative numbers represent cash outflows (money leaving your pocket—investments, payments) - Positive numbers represent cash inflows (money coming to you—returns, receipts)
Example: If you invest $1,000 today at 5% for 10 years:
=FV(0.05, 10, 0, -1000)
The -1000 represents money leaving your pocket today. The result will be positive (approximately $1,629), representing money coming back to you in the future.
2. Interest Rates as Decimals
Always enter interest rates as decimals: - 5% = 0.05 - 7.5% = 0.075 - 12% = 0.12
3. Period Consistency
Ensure your interest rate period matches your time period: - Annual rate (6%) with annual periods (10 years) ✓ - Monthly rate (0.5%) with monthly periods (120 months) ✓ - Annual rate (6%) with monthly periods (120 months) ✗ WRONG
4. The [type] Parameter
The [type] parameter specifies when payments occur: - type = 0 (or omitted): End of period (default) - type = 1: Beginning of period (annuity due)
For single lump-sum problems in this module, omit [type]; Excel defaults to end-of-period (type = 0). In Module 2 when we cover annuities, we’ll explicitly use type = 1 for annuity due situations (like rent payments made at the beginning of each month).


2.8.3 Excel Function Examples
Example 1: Future Value You invest $2,000 today at 4% for 5 years. What will you have?
=FV(0.04, 5, 0, -2000)
Result: $2,433.31

Example 2: Present Value You need $25,000 in 10 years. How much must you invest today at 7%?
=PV(0.07, 10, 0, -25000)
Result: $12,715.64

Example 3: Interest Rate You invested $5,000 and it grew to $8,000 in 7 years. What was your annual return?
=RATE(7, 0, -5000, 8000)
Result: 0.0701 or 7.01%

Example 4: Number of Periods How long will it take $10,000 to grow to $20,000 at 6%?
=NPER(0.06, 0, -10000, 20000)
Result: 11.90 years
Verification with Rule of 72: 72 ÷ 6 = 12 years (very close!)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.9 Preview: Lump Sums vs. Annuities
In this module, we’ve focused exclusively on lump sum problems—single amounts of money at specific points in time.
Lump Sum Examples: - Investing $5,000 today in a savings account - Receiving a $10,000 inheritance in 3 years - A bond’s final principal payment at maturity - Winning a lottery prize paid as a single amount

2.9.1 What’s Coming in Module 2
In Module 2: Discounted Cash Flows, we’ll extend these concepts to annuities—regular, repeated payments over time.
Annuity Examples: - Monthly car payments ($350/month for 60 months) - Annual retirement account contributions ($6,000/year for 30 years) - Quarterly bond interest payments ($30/quarter for 10 years) - Mortgage payments ($1,500/month for 360 months)
The Connection: Annuities are just multiple lump sums strung together. Everything you’ve learned about PV and FV applies—we’ll just add the PMT (payment) parameter to our Excel functions.
Sneak Preview of Excel Functions for Annuities: - =PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv]) — We’ll use the PMT parameter to find present value of payment streams - =PMT(rate, nper, pv, [fv]) — Solves for the payment amount (e.g., “What’s my monthly car payment?”) - =FV(rate, nper, pmt, [pv]) — Future value of regular payments (e.g., “How much will my retirement account be worth?”)
Don’t worry about mastering these now—just know that the TVM foundation you’re building in Module 1 applies to all future modules. Once you understand how to move a single dollar through time, moving multiple dollars through time is just an extension of the same logic.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.10 Key Takeaways
By the end of this module, you should understand:
✓ The three pillars of TVM: Money today is worth more than money tomorrow due to opportunity cost, inflation, and risk
✓ Compounding creates exponential growth: “Interest on interest” is what allows small savings to grow into large sums over long periods
✓ FV moves forward; PV discounts backward: These are inverse operations along the time continuum
✓ The four core variables: PV, FV, r (rate), and n (periods)—know three, solve for the fourth
✓ Excel functions streamline TVM work: Master FV, PV, RATE, and NPER with proper sign conventions
✓ Compounding frequency matters: APY (effective rate) differs from APR (nominal rate) when compounding is more frequent than annual
✓ The Rule of 72: A powerful mental shortcut for estimating doubling time (Years ≈ 72 ÷ Rate)
✓ Discount rates reflect risk: Higher risk requires higher returns; the discount rate = risk-free rate + inflation premium + risk premium
✓ Time is your most valuable asset: Starting early with small amounts beats starting late with large amounts due to the exponential nature of compounding
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.11 Common Misconceptions and Pitfalls

2.11.1 Misconception 1: “Compound interest is only slightly better than simple interest”
Reality: Over long periods, the difference is enormous. For $10,000 at 8% over 30 years: - Simple interest: $34,000 - Compound interest: $100,627 - Difference: $66,627 (nearly 3x the original investment!)


2.11.2 Misconception 2: “I can wait a few years to start saving for retirement”
Reality: Due to compounding, delaying even 5 years can cost you hundreds of thousands of dollars. The Rule of 72 shows why: each doubling period you miss is exponentially costly.


2.11.3 Misconception 3: “A higher APR is always worse for borrowers”
Reality: You must compare APY, not APR. A 5.9% APR compounded daily might cost more than a 6.0% APR compounded annually.


2.11.4 Misconception 4: “Present value and future value are just different names for the same thing”
Reality: They move in opposite directions. PV discounts future money to today; FV compounds present money to the future. They’re mathematical inverses.


2.11.5 Misconception 5: “The Rule of 72 is just a rough guess”
Reality: For rates between 6-10%, the Rule of 72 is accurate to within a few months. It’s based on solid logarithmic mathematics, just simplified for mental calculation.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)
text:
2.12 Connecting to Future Modules
The TVM concepts you’ve mastered in in this module are the foundation for everything that follows:
Discounted Cash Flows - Extends lump sums to annuities (regular payments) - Introduces perpetuities (payments that continue forever) - Applies TVM to loan amortization and retirement planning
Bond Valuation - Bonds are just a series of annuity payments (coupons) plus a lump sum (principal) - Bond prices move inversely with interest rates—now you understand why
Stock Valuation - Dividend discount models are PV calculations applied to uncertain future cash flows - The Gordon Growth Model is a perpetuity formula with growth
Risk and Return - Understanding how to measure investment risk using variance and standard deviation - Learning how the risk premium component of discount rates is determined - Applying the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) to estimate required returns based on systematic risk
Everything in finance is about moving cash flows through time. Master this, and you’ve mastered the language of finance.
crumbs: 2  The Time Value of Money (TVM)

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.1 Moving Beyond Lump Sums
In Module 1, we focused on lump sums—single cash flows occurring at a specific point in time. However, most financial decisions involve multiple cash flows. Whether analyzing a business’s monthly revenue, a series of car payments, or retirement savings contributions, you are dealing with Discounted Cash Flows (DCF).
The core principle remains unchanged: to compare or aggregate cash flows occurring at different times, you must move them to the same point in time (usually the present) using a discount rate.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
In Practice: The “Valuation” Mindset
In finance, the value of any financial asset—a stock, a bond, or an entire company—is simply the present value of all the cash it is expected to generate in the future. If you can forecast the cash flows and choose an appropriate discount rate, you can calculate the “fair” price of anything. This is why DCF analysis is the foundation of investment banking, private equity, and corporate finance.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.2 Defining Annuities
An annuity is a series of equal payments occurring at fixed intervals for a specified number of periods. Annuities simplify valuation because the equal payment structure allows us to use closed-form formulas rather than valuing each cash flow individually.

3.2.1 The Two Types of Annuities

Ordinary Annuity: Payments occur at the end of each period. This is the default assumption in finance (e.g., most corporate bond interest payments, mortgage payments).
Annuity Due: Payments occur at the beginning of each period (e.g., rent payments, lease payments, insurance premiums).



3.2.2 Mathematical Intuition
The Present Value of an annuity is the sum of the present values of each individual payment. Rather than discounting each payment separately, we use a formula that captures this sum efficiently.
Ordinary Annuity Formula:
\[PV = PMT \times \left[ \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \right]\]
Where: - \(PMT\) = the payment amount per period - \(r\) = the discount rate per period - \(n\) = the number of periods
Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity:
\[FV = PMT \times \left[ \frac{(1 + r)^{n} - 1}{r} \right]\]
Annuity Due Adjustment:
Because payments occur one period earlier, an Annuity Due is worth more than an Ordinary Annuity:
\[PV_{due} = PV_{ordinary} \times (1 + r)\]
\[FV_{due} = FV_{ordinary} \times (1 + r)\]

In Practice: Why Timing Matters
Consider rent versus a car loan. Rent is typically an Annuity Due (you pay at the start of the month to secure housing for that month). A car loan is typically an Ordinary Annuity (you drive for a month, then make the payment at month’s end). Because money has time value, an Annuity Due is always worth more than an identical Ordinary Annuity—you receive (or pay) the money sooner, which means one less period of discounting.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.3 Perpetuities: The “Forever” Cash Flow
A perpetuity is an annuity that continues forever. While “forever” sounds theoretical, it is a vital concept for valuing preferred stocks, real estate with stable rental income, and established companies with predictable cash flows.
The Formula:
\[PV = \frac{PMT}{r}\]
This elegant formula emerges from the ordinary annuity formula as \(n \to \infty\). The \((1 + r)^{-n}\) term approaches zero, leaving us with \(\frac{PMT}{r}\).

3.3.1 Growing Perpetuity
When cash flows grow at a constant rate \(g\) forever, we use the Growing Perpetuity Formula:
\[PV = \frac{PMT}{r - g}\]
Critical constraint: \(g < r\). If the growth rate equals or exceeds the discount rate, the formula breaks down, reflecting the economic reality that no entity can grow faster than the economy indefinitely.

In Practice: Real-World Perpetuities
The most famous example is the British Consol, a bond issued by the UK government that has no maturity date and pays interest forever. In corporate finance, we use the perpetuity formula to calculate the “Terminal Value” of a company—the value of all cash flows beyond a 5 or 10-year forecast horizon. For example, if a company is expected to generate $10 million in free cash flow annually starting in Year 6, and your discount rate is 10%, the present value of all those future cash flows is \(\frac{10,000,000}{0.10} = \$100,000,000\) (as of Year 5), which you then discount back to today.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.4 Excel Functions for Annuities
In Module 1, we used =PV() and =FV() for lump sums. In Module 2, we introduce the PMT (payment) argument to handle annuities.

3.4.1 The Five Variables
Most DCF problems involve five variables. If you know any four, Excel can solve for the fifth:

Rate (\(r\)): The interest rate per period
Nper (\(n\)): The total number of payment periods
PV: The present value (current worth)
FV: The future value (value at the end)
PMT: The constant payment made each period



3.4.2 Essential Excel Functions
To find the payment:
=PMT(rate, nper, pv, [fv], [type])
To find the number of periods:
=NPER(rate, pmt, pv, [fv], [type])
To find the interest rate:
=RATE(nper, pmt, pv, [fv], [type])
To find the present value:
=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type])
To find the future value:
=FV(rate, nper, pmt, [pv], [type])
Note on [type]: - Use 0 (or omit) for an Ordinary Annuity (payments at end of period) - Use 1 for an Annuity Due (payments at beginning of period)

In Practice: The Sign Convention (Again!)
Excel treats these as cash inflows and outflows. If you are calculating the payment for a $30,000 car loan at 6% annual interest for 5 years, the PV is \(+30,000\) (money coming to you from the lender), and the resulting PMT will be negative (money leaving your pocket each month). The formula would be: =PMT(0.06/12, 5*12, 30000, 0, 0). If you enter both PV and PMT as positive, Excel will return an error or nonsensical result because it violates the cash flow direction logic.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.5 Loan Amortization
A loan amortization schedule breaks down each payment into its principal and interest components. This is critical for understanding how loans work and for tax purposes (interest is often tax-deductible).

3.5.1 The Mechanics
Each payment is constant (assuming a fixed-rate loan), but the composition changes: - Interest portion = Outstanding balance × Periodic interest rate - Principal portion = Total payment − Interest portion - New balance = Previous balance − Principal portion
Over time, the interest portion decreases and the principal portion increases, because you owe less with each payment.


3.5.2 Excel Implementation
You can build an amortization schedule using: - =PMT() to calculate the constant payment - =IPMT(rate, per, nper, pv) to calculate interest for a specific period - =PPMT(rate, per, nper, pv) to calculate principal for a specific period

In Practice: The True Cost of Borrowing
Consider a $300,000 mortgage at 4% annual interest for 30 years. Your monthly payment is $1,432.25. Over 30 years, you will pay $515,609 total—meaning you paid $215,609 in interest alone. Understanding amortization helps you see why making extra principal payments early in the loan dramatically reduces total interest paid, because you’re reducing the balance on which future interest is calculated.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
section: 3.6 Uneven Cash Flows and Net Present Value (NPV)
text:
3.6 Uneven Cash Flows and Net Present Value (NPV)
Not all cash flows are equal. When payments vary from period to period, we cannot use the simple annuity formulas. Instead, we use the Net Present Value (NPV) approach, which sums the present value of each individual cash flow.
Mathematical Formula:
\[NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}\]
Where \(CF_t\) is the cash flow in period \(t\).

3.6.1 Excel Function
=NPV(rate, value1, [value2], ...)
Crucial Warning: The Excel =NPV() function assumes the first value in your range occurs at the end of Period 1, not at Time 0. If you have a cash flow at Time 0 (today)—such as an initial investment—you must exclude it from the NPV function and add it separately:
=Initial_Investment + NPV(rate, future_cash_flows)
Since initial investments are typically cash outflows, they are negative:
=-100000 + NPV(0.10, C2:C6)

In Practice: Capital Budgeting Decisions
Suppose you’re evaluating a project that requires a $500,000 investment today and will generate cash flows of $150,000, $200,000, $180,000, $120,000, and $100,000 over the next five years. If your required return is 12%, the NPV is:
=-500000 + NPV(0.12, {150000, 200000, 180000, 120000, 100000}) = $48,610
A positive NPV means the project creates value and should be accepted. A negative NPV means it destroys value and should be rejected. This is the foundation of corporate investment decisions.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.7 Common Pitfalls to Avoid

3.7.1 1. Rate/Period Mismatch
The most common error in DCF analysis. If payments are monthly, your rate must be monthly and your periods must be in months.

Annual rate of 12% with monthly payments → Use \(r = 0.12/12 = 0.01\) and \(n = years \times 12\)
Quarterly payments → Use \(r = annual\ rate / 4\) and \(n = years \times 4\)



3.7.2 2. Annuity Due Toggle
Forgetting to change the [type] argument to 1 in Excel when payments occur at the beginning of the period. This is especially common with rent and lease problems.


3.7.3 3. NPV Time Zero Error
Using =NPV() for all cash flows including the initial investment. Remember: Excel’s NPV function assumes the first cash flow is at the end of Period 1.


3.7.4 4. Perpetuity Growth Constraint
When using the Growing Perpetuity formula \(PV = \frac{PMT}{r - g}\), the growth rate \(g\) must be less than the discount rate \(r\). If \(g \geq r\), the formula produces nonsensical results (negative or infinite values).


3.7.5 5. Sign Convention Errors
Mixing up cash inflows and outflows. Be consistent: money you receive is positive, money you pay is negative (or vice versa, as long as you’re consistent).
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities
text:
3.8 Connecting to Future Modules
In this module, we have mastered how to value streams of cash flows using annuities, perpetuities, and NPV for uneven cash flows.

In Bond Valuation, we will apply these DCF techniques to value government and corporate debt. A bond is simply an annuity (coupon payments) plus a lump sum (par value at maturity).
In Stock Valuation, we will use perpetuities and growing perpetuities to determine the intrinsic value of equity using dividend discount models.
In Risk and Return, we will finally learn how to determine the “correct” discount rate (\(r\)) to use in all these calculations based on the risk of the investment.

The DCF framework you’ve learned here is the foundation of all valuation in finance. Master these mechanics now, and the rest of the course will build naturally on this foundation.
crumbs: 3  Discounted Cash Flows and Annuities

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.1 Module Overview
Now that you understand how to value single cash flows and streams of cash flows like annuities, you’re ready to apply these skills to one of the most important financial instruments: bonds.
A bond is essentially a loan you make to a borrower (a corporation or government). In return, the borrower promises to: 1. Pay you regular interest payments (called coupons) 2. Return your principal (called par value or face value) at maturity
Why bonds matter: Bonds are the foundation of corporate finance and investment management. Companies issue bonds to raise capital, investors buy bonds for steady income, and bond prices signal market expectations about interest rates and economic conditions. Understanding bond valuation is essential for capital budgeting, cost of capital calculations, and portfolio management.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.2 Bond Basics: The Mechanics

4.2.1 What Is a Bond?
Think of a bond as an IOU with specific terms:

Par Value (Face Value): The amount the bond will pay at maturity (typically $1,000)
Coupon Rate: The annual interest rate stated on the bond
Coupon Payment: The dollar amount of interest paid periodically (usually semi-annually)
Maturity Date: When the bond expires and par value is repaid
Yield to Maturity (YTM): The market’s required return on the bond (the discount rate)



4.2.2 Example Bond
Consider a 5-year, 6% annual coupon bond with a $1,000 par value:

Par Value: $1,000
Coupon Rate: 6% per year
Annual Coupon Payment: $1,000 × 0.06 = $60
Maturity: 5 years
Cash Flows: You receive $60 at the end of each year for 5 years, plus $1,000 at year 5

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.3 The Bond Valuation Formula
A bond is simply a combination of two cash flow patterns you already know:

An annuity (the coupon payments)
A lump sum (the par value at maturity)


4.3.1 The Formula
\[\text{Bond Price} = \text{PV of Coupons} + \text{PV of Par Value}\]
\[P = C \times \left[\frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}\]
Where: - \(P\) = Bond price (present value) - \(C\) = Coupon payment per period - \(r\) = Yield to maturity (YTM) per period (market interest rate) - \(n\) = Number of periods until maturity - \(F\) = Face (par) value


4.3.2 Worked Example
Given: - Par value = $1,000 - Coupon rate = 6% (annual) - Maturity = 5 years - Market YTM = 8%
Step 1: Calculate annual coupon payment \[C = \$1,000 \times 0.06 = \$60\]
Step 2: Calculate PV of coupon payments (annuity) \[PV_{\text{coupons}} = \$60 \times \left[\frac{1-(1.08)^{-5}}{0.08}\right] = \$60 \times 3.9927 = \$239.56\]
Step 3: Calculate PV of par value (lump sum) \[PV_{\text{par}} = \frac{\$1,000}{(1.08)^5} = \frac{\$1,000}{1.4693} = \$680.58\]
Step 4: Add them together \[\text{Bond Price} = \$239.56 + \$680.58 = \$920.14\]
Interpretation: This bond is selling at a discount (below par value) because the market YTM (8%) is higher than the coupon rate (6%). Investors demand a higher return than the bond’s coupon provides, so the price must fall to make up the difference.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
section: 4.4 The Inverse Relationship: Interest Rates and Bond Prices
text:
4.4 The Inverse Relationship: Interest Rates and Bond Prices

4.4.1 The Golden Rule of Bonds
When market interest rates rise, bond prices fall. When market interest rates fall, bond prices rise.
This is the most important concept in bond valuation.


4.4.2 Why This Happens
Imagine you own a bond paying 6% when market rates are 6%—your bond is worth par value ($1,000). Now suppose market rates jump to 8%:

New bonds issued today pay 8%
Your bond still only pays 6%
No one will pay $1,000 for your 6% bond when they can buy a new 8% bond for $1,000
Your bond’s price must fall to make its total return competitive with the 8% market rate

In practice: This is why bond investors fear rising interest rates. If you bought a 30-year Treasury bond in 2020 when rates were 1.5%, and rates rose to 4.5% by 2023, your bond’s market value dropped significantly—even though the bond itself is perfectly safe and will still pay you back at maturity.


4.4.3 Three Pricing Scenarios



Relationship
Bond Price
Term




Coupon Rate > YTM
Price > Par
Premium Bond


Coupon Rate = YTM
Price = Par
Par Bond


Coupon Rate < YTM
Price < Par
Discount Bond

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.5 Semi-Annual Coupon Bonds
Most bonds in the real world pay interest semi-annually (twice per year), not annually. This requires adjusting our formula.

4.5.1 Adjustments Needed

Divide the annual coupon payment by 2 to get the semi-annual payment
Divide the annual YTM by 2 to get the semi-annual rate
Multiply the years to maturity by 2 to get the number of semi-annual periods



4.5.2 Example: Semi-Annual Bond
Given: - Par value = $1,000 - Annual coupon rate = 6% - Maturity = 5 years - Annual YTM = 8%
Adjustments: - Semi-annual coupon = ($1,000 × 0.06) ÷ 2 = $30 - Semi-annual YTM = 0.08 ÷ 2 = 0.04 - Number of periods = 5 × 2 = 10
Formula: \[P = \$30 \times \left[\frac{1-(1.04)^{-10}}{0.04}\right] + \frac{\$1,000}{(1.04)^{10}}\]
\[P = \$30 \times 8.1109 + \$675.56 = \$243.33 + \$675.56 = \$918.89\]
Note: The semi-annual bond price ($918.89) is slightly lower than the annual bond price ($920.14) because compounding happens more frequently.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.6 Yield to Maturity (YTM)

4.6.1 What Is YTM?
Yield to Maturity is the total return you’ll earn if you: 1. Buy the bond at its current market price 2. Hold it until maturity 3. Reinvest all coupon payments at the YTM rate
YTM is the discount rate that makes the present value of all bond cash flows equal to the current bond price.


4.6.2 Why YTM Matters

YTM is the bond’s true rate of return
It allows you to compare bonds with different coupons, maturities, and prices
It’s the market’s assessment of the bond’s risk and return



4.6.3 Calculating YTM
Unlike calculating bond price (where we know YTM and solve for price), calculating YTM requires solving for the rate when we know the price. This requires trial and error or Excel.
Formula (cannot be solved algebraically): \[\text{Current Price} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+YTM)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+YTM)^n}\]
In practice: Bond traders and portfolio managers constantly monitor YTM because it reflects changing market conditions. If a company’s credit quality deteriorates, its bond YTM will rise (and price will fall) as investors demand higher returns for increased risk.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
section: 4.7 Current Yield vs. Yield to Maturity
text:
4.7 Current Yield vs. Yield to Maturity

4.7.1 Current Yield
Current Yield is a simpler measure that only considers the annual coupon income relative to the current price:
\[\text{Current Yield} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon Payment}}{\text{Current Bond Price}}\]


4.7.2 Example
For our bond priced at $920.14 with a \(60 annual coupon:\)\(\text{Current Yield} = \frac{\$60}{\$920.14} = 0.0652 = 6.52\%\)$


4.7.3 Current Yield vs. YTM

Current Yield only measures coupon income (ignores capital gain/loss at maturity)
YTM measures total return (coupon income + capital gain/loss)
For discount bonds: YTM > Current Yield > Coupon Rate
For premium bonds: Coupon Rate > Current Yield > YTM
For par bonds: All three are equal

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.8 Types of Bonds: Corporate Bonds

4.8.1 What Are Corporate Bonds?
Corporate bonds are debt securities issued by companies to raise capital for operations, expansion, or acquisitions. When you buy a corporate bond, you’re lending money to the company.


4.8.2 Key Features

Default Risk: Unlike government bonds, corporations can go bankrupt and fail to repay
Credit Ratings: Agencies like Standard & Poor’s (S&P) and Moody’s rate corporate bonds based on default risk
Higher Yields: Corporate bonds pay higher interest rates than government bonds to compensate for default risk

4.8.4 Investment Grade vs. Junk Bonds

Investment Grade (BBB-/Baa3 or higher): Lower risk, lower yields, suitable for conservative investors
Junk Bonds (Below BBB-/Baa3): Higher risk, higher yields, also called “high-yield bonds”



4.8.5 Bond Seniority
Corporate bonds have different priority levels in case of bankruptcy:

Senior Secured Bonds: Backed by specific collateral (highest priority)
Senior Unsecured Bonds: Not backed by collateral but have priority over subordinated debt
Subordinated Bonds: Lower priority, higher risk, higher yield

In practice: During the 2008 financial crisis, many corporate bonds were downgraded from investment grade to junk status. Lehman Brothers’ bonds, once rated A, became worthless when the firm collapsed. This demonstrates why credit risk matters—even bonds from major corporations can default.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.9 Types of Bonds: Government Bonds (Treasuries)

4.9.1 What Are Treasury Securities?
U.S. Treasury securities are debt obligations issued by the federal government. They are considered the safest investments in the world because they’re backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.


4.9.2 Types of Treasuries

4.9.2.1 1. Treasury Bills (T-Bills)

Maturity: Less than 1 year (4, 8, 13, 26, or 52 weeks)
Structure: Sold at a discount, no coupon payments
Example: Buy a $1,000 T-Bill for $980, receive $1,000 at maturity



4.9.2.2 2. Treasury Notes (T-Notes)

Maturity: 2, 3, 5, 7, or 10 years
Structure: Pay semi-annual coupon interest
Most Popular: The 10-year Treasury note is the benchmark for mortgage rates and corporate bonds



4.9.2.3 3. Treasury Bonds (T-Bonds)

Maturity: 20 or 30 years
Structure: Pay semi-annual coupon interest
Use: Long-term savings, pension funds, insurance companies




4.9.3 Why Treasuries Matter

Risk-Free Rate: Treasury yields represent the “risk-free rate” used in financial models
Benchmark: All other bonds are priced relative to Treasuries
Economic Indicator: Treasury yields signal market expectations about inflation and economic growth



4.9.4 The Yield Curve
The yield curve plots Treasury yields against maturity:

Normal Curve: Longer maturities have higher yields (upward sloping)
Inverted Curve: Shorter maturities have higher yields (downward sloping)—often predicts recession
Flat Curve: Similar yields across maturities—signals economic uncertainty

In practice: The 10-year Treasury yield is watched obsessively by financial markets. When it rises, mortgage rates increase, corporate borrowing becomes more expensive, and stock valuations often fall. When it falls, it signals investors are seeking safety or expecting economic weakness.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.10 Types of Bonds: Municipal Bonds

4.10.1 What Are Municipal Bonds?
Municipal bonds (munis) are issued by state and local governments to finance public projects like schools, highways, water systems, and hospitals.


4.10.2 The Tax Advantage
The key feature of municipal bonds is tax-exempt interest:

Interest income is exempt from federal income tax
Often exempt from state and local taxes if you live in the issuing state
This makes munis attractive to high-income investors in high tax brackets



4.10.3 Types of Municipal Bonds

4.10.3.1 1. General Obligation Bonds (GO Bonds)

Backed by the full taxing power of the issuing government
Considered safer
Lower yields



4.10.3.2 2. Revenue Bonds

Backed by revenue from a specific project (toll road, airport, water system)
Higher risk than GO bonds
Higher yields




4.10.4 Taxable Equivalent Yield
To compare municipal bonds to taxable bonds, calculate the taxable equivalent yield:
\[\text{Taxable Equivalent Yield} = \frac{\text{Tax-Free Yield}}{1 - \text{Tax Rate}}\]


4.10.5 Example
You’re in the 35% federal tax bracket and considering: - A municipal bond yielding 4% (tax-free) - A corporate bond yielding 6% (taxable)
Calculate taxable equivalent yield: \[\text{TEY} = \frac{0.04}{1 - 0.35} = \frac{0.04}{0.65} = 0.0615 = 6.15\%\]
Interpretation: The 4% muni bond is equivalent to a 6.15% taxable bond. Since the corporate bond only yields 6%, the muni bond is the better choice for this investor.


4.10.6 Who Should Buy Munis?

High tax bracket investors: The tax benefit is most valuable
Low tax bracket investors: Taxable bonds often provide better after-tax returns
Tax-deferred accounts (IRAs, 401(k)s): Never buy munis here—you lose the tax advantage

In practice: After the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act lowered federal tax rates, municipal bonds became less attractive to some investors because the tax benefit decreased. This shows how tax policy directly impacts bond markets.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.11 Excel Functions for Bond Valuation
Excel provides powerful built-in functions for bond calculations.

4.11.1 Function 1: =PV() for Bond Pricing
Syntax:
=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type])
Example: Price a 5-year, 6% annual coupon bond with $1,000 par value when YTM = 8%
=PV(0.08, 5, 60, 1000, 0)
Result: -$920.14 (negative because it’s a cash outflow)
Note: Always enter PMT and FV with the same sign (both positive or both negative) and PV will have the opposite sign.



4.11.2 Function 2: =PRICE() for Bond Pricing
Syntax:
=PRICE(settlement, maturity, rate, yld, redemption, frequency, [basis])
Parameters: - settlement = Purchase date - maturity = Maturity date - rate = Annual coupon rate - yld = Annual yield to maturity - redemption = Redemption value per $100 face value (usually 100) - frequency = Coupon payments per year (1 = annual, 2 = semi-annual) - basis = Day count convention (usually 0 or omitted)
Example: Price a bond purchased on 1/15/2026, maturing 1/15/2031, 6% coupon, 8% YTM, semi-annual payments
=PRICE(DATE(2026,1,15), DATE(2031,1,15), 0.06, 0.08, 100, 2)
Result: 92.014 (meaning $920.14 per $1,000 face value)



4.11.3 Function 3: =YIELD() for YTM Calculation
Syntax:
=YIELD(settlement, maturity, rate, pr, redemption, frequency, [basis])
Example: Calculate YTM for a bond purchased at $920.14, 6% coupon, 5 years to maturity, semi-annual payments
=YIELD(DATE(2026,1,15), DATE(2031,1,15), 0.06, 92.014, 100, 2)
Result: 0.08 or 8%



4.11.4 Function 4: =RATE() for YTM (Alternative Method)
Syntax:
=RATE(nper, pmt, pv, [fv], [type])
Example: Calculate YTM for a bond priced at $920.14, paying $60 annually for 5 years, $1,000 par value
=RATE(5, 60, -920.14, 1000)
Result: 0.08 or 8%
Note: For semi-annual bonds, multiply the result by 2 to get the annual YTM.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.12 Bond Pricing Relationships: Key Principles

4.12.1 Principle 1: Inverse Price-Yield Relationship

When YTM ↑, bond price ↓
When YTM ↓, bond price ↑



4.12.2 Principle 2: Longer Maturity = Greater Price Sensitivity

A 30-year bond’s price changes more than a 5-year bond’s price for the same change in YTM
This is called interest rate risk or duration risk



4.12.3 Principle 3: Lower Coupon = Greater Price Sensitivity

Zero-coupon bonds are most sensitive to interest rate changes
High-coupon bonds are less sensitive



4.12.4 Principle 4: Price Converges to Par at Maturity

As maturity approaches, bond price moves toward par value
At maturity, price = par value (regardless of coupon rate)

In practice: This is why bond portfolio managers carefully match bond durations to their investment horizons. A pension fund with liabilities 20 years out will buy long-term bonds, while a money market fund needing liquidity will stick to short-term bonds.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.13 Real-World Applications

4.13.1 Application 1: Corporate Borrowing Decisions
When a company decides to issue bonds, it must consider: - Credit rating: Better ratings = lower interest costs - Maturity: Longer maturity = more interest rate risk but locked-in rates - Coupon structure: Fixed vs. floating rate
Example: In 2020, Apple issued $8.5 billion in bonds at historically low rates (some as low as 0.75%) to take advantage of the low-interest-rate environment, even though Apple has massive cash reserves.


4.13.2 Application 2: Investment Portfolio Construction
Investors use bonds for: - Income generation: Steady coupon payments - Capital preservation: Less volatile than stocks - Diversification: Bonds often rise when stocks fall
Example: A retiree might hold 60% stocks and 40% bonds, with the bonds providing stable income and reducing overall portfolio volatility.


4.13.3 Application 3: Economic Forecasting
Bond markets signal economic expectations: - Rising Treasury yields: Expectations of economic growth and/or inflation - Falling Treasury yields: Expectations of economic slowdown or deflation - Widening credit spreads: Increasing concern about corporate defaults
Example: In March 2020, Treasury yields plummeted as investors fled to safety during the COVID-19 pandemic, while corporate bond spreads widened dramatically as default risk increased.

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.14 Wrap-Up

4.14.1 What You’ve Learned
✓ Bonds are combinations of annuities (coupons) and lump sums (par value)
✓ Bond prices and interest rates move in opposite directions
✓ YTM is the true measure of a bond’s return
✓ Corporate bonds carry default risk and are rated by credit agencies
✓ Treasury securities are risk-free and serve as market benchmarks
✓ Municipal bonds offer tax advantages for high-income investors
✓ Excel provides powerful functions for bond valuation


4.14.2 Looking Ahead: Stock Valuation
In the next module, we’ll apply these valuation principles to stocks. Unlike bonds with fixed cash flows, stocks have uncertain dividends and no maturity date. You’ll learn:

How to value stocks using dividend discount models
The difference between preferred and common stock
Why P/E ratios matter
How growth expectations affect stock prices



4.14.3 Connection to Risk and Return
Understanding bond valuation is essential for Module 5, where we’ll explore:

How bond yields reflect risk premiums
The relationship between bond duration and interest rate risk
Why diversification between stocks and bonds reduces portfolio risk
How to construct efficient portfolios using the risk-return tradeoff

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.15 Key Formulas Summary
Bond Price (Annual Coupons): \[P = C \times \left[\frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+r)^n}\]
Current Yield: \[\text{Current Yield} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon Payment}}{\text{Current Bond Price}}\]
Taxable Equivalent Yield: \[\text{TEY} = \frac{\text{Tax-Free Yield}}{1 - \text{Tax Rate}}\]

title: 4  Bond Valuation
text:
4.16 Practice Recommendations
Before moving to the Concept Check questions and Excel problems:

Verify your understanding: Can you explain why bond prices fall when interest rates rise?
Practice the mechanics: Work through the bond pricing example by hand
Master Excel functions: Try =PV() and =PRICE() with different inputs
Apply to real bonds: Look up current Treasury yields at treasurydirect.gov
Compare bond types: Calculate taxable equivalent yields for your tax bracket

Remember: Bond valuation is a foundational skill for corporate finance, investment analysis, and portfolio management. The time you invest in mastering these concepts will pay dividends throughout your MBA program and career. I could have said “coupons” instead of dividends, but that would have been a terrible dad joke.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
5.1 Module Overview
In the previous module we valued bonds—debt instruments with contractually promised cash flows. In Module 4, we shift to equity securities (stocks)—ownership claims with uncertain future cash flows and no maturity date.
Key Difference: Bondholders have a legal claim to fixed payments. Stockholders have a residual claim on whatever is left after all other obligations are met. This uncertainty makes stock valuation both more challenging and more interesting.
What You’ll Learn:
Connection to Previous Modules:

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
The fundamental characteristics of common and preferred stock
How to value stocks using the Dividend Discount Model (DDM)
The Gordon Growth Model for constant-growth stocks
Multi-stage growth models for high-growth companies
Relative valuation using P/E ratios and multiples
Excel techniques for stock valuation



Discounted Cash Flows: Stock valuation is DCF applied to uncertain equity cash flows
Bonds: Preferred stock behaves like a perpetual bond
Risk & Return: We’ll learn how to calculate the required return (\(R\)) using CAPM

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
6.1 What Is Common Stock?
Common stock represents residual ownership in a corporation. When you buy a share of Apple or Tesla, you become a part-owner of that company.
Key Rights and Features:

Voting Rights: Elect the board of directors (typically 1 vote per share)
Residual Claim: Last in line during bankruptcy (after employees, suppliers, bondholders)
Dividends: Discretionary payments—management decides whether and how much to pay
Capital Gains: Profit from selling the stock at a higher price than you paid
Limited Liability: You can only lose your investment, not more

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
6.2 Stockholders vs. Bondholders



Feature
Bondholders
Stockholders




Claim Type
Fixed contractual claim
Residual claim


Payment Priority
Paid first
Paid last


Maturity
Fixed maturity date
No maturity (perpetual)


Return
Interest (coupon)
Dividends + Capital Gains


Risk
Lower risk
Higher risk


Voting Rights
None
Yes









NoteThe Risk-Return Tradeoff



Because stockholders bear more risk than bondholders, they demand higher expected returns. Historically, stocks have returned ~10% annually while bonds have returned ~5-6%. This “equity risk premium” compensates investors for the uncertainty of stock ownership.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
7.1 The Fundamental Principle
The value of any financial asset equals the present value of its expected future cash flows. For stocks, those cash flows are dividends.
If you hold a stock forever, the only cash you receive are dividend payments. Therefore:
\[P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_t}{(1+R)^t}\]
Where:

\(P_0\) = Stock price today
\(D_t\) = Dividend expected at time \(t\)
\(R\) = Required rate of return (discount rate)

The Challenge: Unlike bonds with known coupon payments, we must forecast future dividends, which are uncertain and discretionary.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
7.2 Zero-Growth Model (Preferred Stock)
The simplest case: dividends remain constant forever. This applies to preferred stock, which pays a fixed dividend like a perpetual bond.
\[P_0 = \frac{D}{R}\]
Example: A preferred stock pays a \(\$5\) annual dividend. If your required return is 8%, what is the stock worth?
\[P_0 = \frac{\$5}{0.08} = \$62.50\]






TipIn Practice: Preferred Stock



Preferred stock is a hybrid security—it has characteristics of both bonds (fixed payments) and stocks (no maturity, dividends can be suspended). Companies like banks and utilities issue preferred stock to raise capital without diluting common shareholders’ voting rights.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
8.1 The Formula
For companies that pay dividends growing at a constant rate (\(g\)) forever:
\[P_0 = \frac{D_1}{R - g}\]
Where:

\(D_1\) = Dividend expected next year
\(R\) = Required rate of return
\(g\) = Constant growth rate of dividends

Critical Assumptions:

\(R > g\) (required return must exceed growth rate, or the formula breaks)
Growth rate is constant forever
The company pays dividends

title: 5  Stock Valuation
section: 8.2 Example: Valuing a Mature Dividend Stock
text:
8.2 Example: Valuing a Mature Dividend Stock
Apple Inc. (AAPL) currently pays an annual dividend of \(\$0.96\) per share. Analysts expect Apple’s dividends to grow at 5% per year indefinitely. If your required return is 10%, what is Apple’s stock worth?
Given:

\(D_0 = \$0.96\) (current dividend)
\(g = 5\% = 0.05\)
\(R = 10\% = 0.10\)

Step 1: Calculate next year’s dividend:
\[D_1 = D_0 \times (1 + g) = \$0.96 \times 1.05 = \$1.008\]
Step 2: Apply the Gordon Growth Model:
\[P_0 = \frac{D_1}{R - g} = \frac{\$1.008}{0.10 - 0.05} = \frac{\$1.008}{0.05} = \$20.16\]
Interpretation: Based on this model, Apple’s intrinsic value is \(\$20.16\) per share. If Apple trades at \(\$180\) in the market, either:

Our assumptions are wrong (growth rate too low, required return too high), or
The market is pricing in factors beyond just dividends (like share buybacks and future growth)







ImportantIn the News: Apple’s Capital Return Strategy



Apple is one of the world’s largest dividend payers, returning over \(\$100\) billion annually to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. While Apple’s dividend yield is modest (~0.5%), the company’s massive cash flow generation makes it a favorite among income-focused investors. The Gordon Growth Model works well for Apple because it’s a mature company with stable, predictable cash flows.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
8.3 Solving for Required Return
We can rearrange the Gordon Growth Model to solve for the required return:
\[R = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g\]
This shows that the required return has two components:

Dividend Yield: \(\frac{D_1}{P_0}\) (current income)
Capital Gains Yield: \(g\) (expected price appreciation)

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
9.1 The Problem with Constant Growth
Most exciting stocks don’t grow at a constant rate forever. High-growth companies like Nvidia (NVDA) or Tesla (TSLA) experience periods of rapid expansion before eventually maturing.
Multi-Stage Growth Model: Combines a period of high growth with eventual transition to stable, mature growth.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
9.2 The Three-Step Process

Forecast dividends during the high-growth period (typically 3-5 years)
Calculate the terminal value at the end of high growth using the Gordon Growth Model
Discount everything back to today using the required return

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
9.3 Example: Two-Stage Growth Model
Scenario: A tech company just paid a \(\$2.00\) dividend. For the next 3 years, dividends will grow at 20% per year. After year 3, growth will slow to 5% forever. Your required return is 12%. What is the stock worth?
Given:

\(D_0 = \$2.00\)
High growth rate: \(g_1 = 20\%\) for 3 years
Stable growth rate: \(g_2 = 5\%\) forever after year 3
\(R = 12\%\)

Step 1: Forecast dividends during high-growth period:
\[D_1 = \$2.00 \times 1.20 = \$2.40\] \[D_2 = \$2.40 \times 1.20 = \$2.88\] \[D_3 = \$2.88 \times 1.20 = \$3.456\]
Step 2: Calculate the terminal value at \(t=3\):
First, find \(D_4\) (first dividend in stable growth phase):
\[D_4 = D_3 \times (1 + g_2) = \$3.456 \times 1.05 = \$3.629\]
Then apply Gordon Growth Model at \(t=3\):
\[P_3 = \frac{D_4}{R - g_2} = \frac{\$3.629}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{\$3.629}{0.07} = \$51.84\]
Step 3: Discount all cash flows to present:
\[P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+R)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+R)^2} + \frac{D_3}{(1+R)^3} + \frac{P_3}{(1+R)^3}\]
\[P_0 = \frac{\$2.40}{1.12} + \frac{\$2.88}{1.12^2} + \frac{\$3.456}{1.12^3} + \frac{\$51.84}{1.12^3}\]
\[P_0 = \$2.14 + \$2.30 + \$2.46 + \$36.89 = \$43.79\]
Answer: The stock is worth \(\$43.79\) today.






ImportantIn the News: Nvidia (NVDA) and the AI Boom



Nvidia is the poster child for multi-stage growth. In 2023-2024, NVDA experienced explosive growth (revenue up 200%+) driven by AI chip demand. Analysts use multi-stage models to value Nvidia:

Stage 1 (2024-2027): 30-50% annual growth as AI infrastructure buildout continues
Stage 2 (2028-2032): 15-20% growth as market matures
Stage 3 (2033+): 5-7% stable growth matching GDP

If you used a constant growth model for Nvidia, you’d either: 1. Undervalue it (using mature growth rate), or 2. Get a nonsensical result (using 30% growth forever violates \(R > g\))
This is why Wall Street analysts use sophisticated multi-stage models for high-growth tech stocks.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
section: 10.1 Problem 1: Many Companies Don’t Pay Dividends
text:
10.1 Problem 1: Many Companies Don’t Pay Dividends
Example: Amazon (AMZN) has never paid a dividend. Does that mean Amazon is worthless? Of course not!
Solution: For non-dividend-paying stocks, we use:

Free Cash Flow (FCF) models: Value the company based on cash available to all investors
Relative valuation: Compare to similar companies using multiples

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
10.2 Problem 2: Share Buybacks
Many companies return cash to shareholders through share repurchases rather than dividends.
Example: Meta (META) announced a \(\$50\) billion buyback program in 2024. Buybacks reduce shares outstanding, increasing earnings per share and (theoretically) stock price.
Implication: Dividend models undervalue companies that prefer buybacks over dividends.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
10.3 Problem 3: Growth Rate Assumptions
Small changes in \(g\) dramatically affect valuation.
Example: Using the Gordon Growth Model with \(D_1 = \$2\), \(R = 10\%\):

If \(g = 4\%\): \(P_0 = \frac{\$2}{0.10 - 0.04} = \$33.33\)
If \(g = 5\%\): \(P_0 = \frac{\$2}{0.10 - 0.05} = \$40.00\)
If \(g = 6\%\): \(P_0 = \frac{\$2}{0.10 - 0.06} = \$50.00\)

A 1% change in growth rate changes valuation by 20-50%!






WarningThe Danger of Overly Optimistic Growth Assumptions



During the dot-com bubble (1999-2000), analysts used unrealistic growth rates to justify sky-high valuations. When growth didn’t materialize, stocks crashed 80-90%. Always stress-test your assumptions with conservative scenarios.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
11.1 The P/E Ratio
The Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio compares a stock’s price to its earnings per share:
\[\text{P/E Ratio} = \frac{\text{Price per Share}}{\text{Earnings per Share (EPS)}}\]
Interpretation:

High P/E: Investors expect high future growth (or the stock is overvalued)
Low P/E: Investors expect low growth (or the stock is undervalued)

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
11.2 Example: Tesla vs. Toyota
Tesla (TSLA):

Price: \(\$250\) per share
EPS: \(\$3.00\)
P/E Ratio: \(\frac{\$250}{\$3.00} = 83.3\)

Toyota (TM):

Price: \(\$180\) per share
EPS: \(\$18.00\)
P/E Ratio: \(\frac{\$180}{\$18.00} = 10.0\)

Question: Why does Tesla trade at 8× Toyota’s P/E multiple?
Answer: Investors aren’t just buying a car company—they’re buying:

Growth potential: Tesla’s revenue is growing 30-50% annually vs. Toyota’s 5%
Technology: Autonomous driving, AI, energy storage
Brand premium: Tesla is seen as an innovation leader







NoteIn the News: The “Magnificent Seven” Premium



In 2024, the “Magnificent Seven” tech stocks (Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, Alphabet, Tesla) traded at an average P/E of 35× vs. 18× for the S&P 500. This “multiple expansion” reflects investor belief that these companies will dominate the AI era. Whether this premium is justified remains hotly debated.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
11.3 Using P/E for Valuation
Comparable Company Analysis:

Find similar companies in the same industry
Calculate their average P/E ratio
Apply that multiple to your company’s earnings

Example: You’re valuing a regional bank with EPS of \(\$5.00\). Comparable banks trade at an average P/E of 12×. Estimated value:
\[\text{Price} = \text{EPS} \times \text{P/E} = \$5.00 \times 12 = \$60.00 \text{ per share}\]

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
11.4 Other Common Multiples

Price-to-Sales (P/S): Useful for unprofitable growth companies
Price-to-Book (P/B): Common for financial companies (banks, insurance)
EV/EBITDA: Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, Amortization (accounts for debt)

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
12.1 Key Functions
1. NPV for Multi-Stage Models
=NPV(rate, value1, value2, ...) + value0
Example: Discount the dividends and terminal value from our earlier two-stage model:
=NPV(0.12, 2.40, 2.88, 3.456+51.84)
2. PV for Perpetuities (Zero-Growth)
=PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type])
For a perpetuity, use a very large nper (e.g., 1000):
=PV(0.08, 1000, 5, 0, 0)
This approximates \(\frac{\$5}{0.08} = \$62.50\)
3. Building the Gordon Growth Model
No built-in function—use cell references:
=D1/(R-g)
Where: - Cell B1 contains \(D_1\) - Cell B2 contains \(R\) - Cell B3 contains \(g\)
Formula: =B1/(B2-B3)

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
13.1 Application 1: Investment Decision-Making
Scenario: You’re considering buying Coca-Cola (KO) at \(\$60\) per share. KO pays a \(\$1.84\) dividend, and you expect 4% annual dividend growth. Your required return is 9%.
Analysis:
\[D_1 = \$1.84 \times 1.04 = \$1.914\]
\[P_0 = \frac{\$1.914}{0.09 - 0.04} = \frac{\$1.914}{0.05} = \$38.28\]
Decision: At \(\$60\), Coca-Cola appears overvalued by 57%. You should not buy unless you believe growth will exceed 4% or your required return should be lower.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
13.2 Application 2: Sector Rotation Strategy
Observation: During economic expansions, growth stocks (high P/E, low dividends) outperform. During recessions, value stocks (low P/E, high dividends) outperform.
Strategy:

Expansion: Overweight tech stocks (Nvidia, Microsoft, Amazon)
Recession: Overweight dividend aristocrats (Procter & Gamble, Johnson & Johnson, Coca-Cola)

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
13.3 Application 3: Detecting Market Bubbles
Warning Signs:

P/E ratios far above historical averages
Investors justifying valuations with “new paradigm” arguments
Dividend yields near historic lows

Example: In 2021, the median S&P 500 P/E reached 30× (vs. 16× historical average). Many stocks traded at 50-100× earnings with no dividends. When interest rates rose in 2022, these stocks crashed 50-80%.






TipIn Practice: The “Fed Model”



Some investors compare the S&P 500 earnings yield (\(\frac{E}{P}\), the inverse of P/E) to the 10-year Treasury yield. When stocks yield less than bonds, it suggests stocks are overvalued. This model has limitations but provides a useful reality check.

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
14.1 What You’ve Learned

Common stock represents residual ownership with uncertain cash flows
The Dividend Discount Model values stocks as the PV of future dividends
The Gordon Growth Model applies to mature, constant-growth companies
Multi-stage models handle high-growth companies that eventually mature
Dividend models have limitations (non-dividend payers, buybacks, growth assumptions)
P/E ratios and multiples provide relative valuation benchmarks
Excel’s NPV and PV functions support stock valuation calculations

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
14.2 Looking Ahead: Risk and Return
In Risk and Return, we’ll answer a critical question: How do we determine the required return (\(R\)) used in stock valuation?
You’ll learn:

How to measure investment risk using standard deviation
The difference between systematic (market) risk and unsystematic (firm-specific) risk
How diversification reduces risk
The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) for estimating required returns
How Beta measures a stock’s sensitivity to market movements

Connection: The \(R\) we’ve been using in the Gordon Growth Model comes from CAPM:
\[R = R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f)\]
Where: - \(R_f\) = Risk-free rate (Treasury yield) - \(\beta\) = Stock’s systematic risk - \(R_m - R_f\) = Market risk premium

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
14.3 Connection to Bonds
Bond valuation skills transfer directly to stock valuation:

Preferred stock is valued like a perpetual bond
Dividend growth is analogous to coupon payments
Required return reflects risk, just like YTM for bonds
Interest rate risk: When rates rise, both bond and stock prices fall

title: 5  Stock Valuation
text:
14.4 Key Formulas Summary
Zero-Growth DDM (Preferred Stock):
\[P_0 = \frac{D}{R}\]
Gordon Growth Model:
\[P_0 = \frac{D_1}{R - g}\]
Required Return (from Gordon Growth Model):
\[R = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g\]
P/E Ratio:
\[\text{P/E} = \frac{\text{Price per Share}}{\text{EPS}}\]
Multi-Stage Model (Two-Stage Example):
\[P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_t}{(1+R)^t} + \frac{P_n}{(1+R)^n}\]
Where \(P_n = \frac{D_{n+1}}{R - g}\) (terminal value)
